text: Lab session: your assigned task doesn't compile and the deadline is in twenty minutes.

choices.label: Ask the TA
results: The TA points out a missing semicolon. You want to disappear.
The TA walks you through it, mostly. You still owe them one.
They lean over, spot the bug, and joke about it. You fix it with five minutes to spare. Perks of having friends.
They discreetly move their computer screen toward you. It's a lifesaver. You owe them a coffee.
They give you a weird look and mumble that they are 'in the zone' and can't help. The silence is deafening.
You lean over to ask, but realize you haven't spoken to them all semester. They pretend not to hear you.

choices.label: Figure it out alone
results: Twenty minutes and several dead ends later, it compiles. You don't know why. You take it.

text: Lab session: your FPGA board won't power on. There is a faint smell of burnt silicon.

choices.label: Buy a new one
results: There goes this month's budget. The board works. You, less so.
They actually let you borrow it. They loudly remind you that you owe them your life, but you survive the lab.
They hand it over with a dramatic sigh. True friendship is sharing hardware in a crisis.
You ask, but they just stare at you. 'I need mine,' they say, turning back to their screen. Brutal.
They laugh, thinking you're joking. When they realize you aren't, the vibe gets incredibly awkward. No board for you.

choices.label: Tell the TA it is broken and don't do the basys3 part of the lab
results: It works. You feel a little guilty. Mostly you feel free.

text: The campus ring is 40 minutes late and you have a demo in 15.

choices.label: Run the last 2km
results: You make it with a minute to spare. Your legs file a formal complaint.
You arrive drenched in sweat right as they call your name. Iconic, in a way.

choices.label: Mail the instructor
results: Not sure if they care but why not.

text: The assignment is due at 12pm. It is currently 1am and you have completed roughly a third of it.

choices.label: Pull an all-nighter
results: You submit at 11:59 pm with the confidence of someone who has seen things.

choices.label: Submit what you have
results: Sometimes you just have to accept your fate and sleep. It is glorious.

text: Your group project teammate has pushed a commit titled 'fixed everything :)'. It has not fixed everything.

choices.label: Rewrite their part yourself
results: It works now. You will never mention this to them. They will never ask.

choices.label: Confront them about it
results: The group chat goes quiet for three hours. Something shifts. Progress, maybe.

choices.label: Leave it like that
results: It was the right call for your mental but not for your potential

text: Math102 instructor Cocan Ketman stares directly at you and asks you to answer the question.

choices.label: Admit you don't know
results: You admit you have no idea. Ketman's silence somehow lands harder than a wrong answer would have.
"Interesting," he says, in the tone of someone who is not interested. The class remembers this for weeks.
Somehow you land it. Ketman gives the smallest possible nod of approval. You'll take it.
You give it a real shot and get it wrong anyway. Ketman moves on without comment, which is somehow worse.

text: A guy from your lecture approaches your desk. He claims he 'already knows coding inside out' and confidently offers to do your CS101 homework for you.

choices.label: Accept his help
results: He takes your laptop, fixes the code instantly, and winks. Easy points, but you feel a strange sense of dread.

choices.label: Ignore him and refuse
results: You tell him you'd rather learn it yourself. He scoffs, murmurs something condescending, and walks away. Good riddance.

text: The 'homework helper' guy is blowing up your phone at 2 AM, sending unsolicited paragraph-long critiques of your code and demanding you grab coffee to discuss it.

choices.label: Mute his notifications
results: You mute him. The next day in the lab, he corners you angrily, asking why you're ignoring him. Your sanity drops.

choices.label: Politely reply to keep the peace
results: You type a polite refusal. He takes this as encouragement and sends five more messages immediately. You can't escape.

text: A friend from your cohort pulls you aside near the department entrance, looking deeply annoyed on your behalf. They tell you: 'I just walked past that guy in the hallway, and he was loudly telling a group that you're totally into him and that you only came to him because you couldn't handle the CS assignments on your own.'

choices.label: Confront him publicly in the next lab
results: You call him out in front of the whole class. He turns bright red, stammers, and completely folds. Everyone looks at him like he's a creep, boosting your social standing, but the drama fries your nerves.

choices.label: Ignore it and let your friends set the record straight
results: You refuse to give him the attention. Your friends go around telling everyone the truth, shutting down his ego, though you still feel a bit frustrated keeping it quiet.

text: You check your account balance before grocery shopping. It is lower than expected. It is always lower than expected.

choices.label: Pasta for the week
results: Efficient. Sad. Effective. You have become a cliche and you know it.

choices.label: Ask family for a top-up
results: They send it, along with several questions about your life choices.

choices.label: Just starve
results: Not sure if that was the best idea
